sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A biochemist studies the hydrolysis of ATP under standard biochemical conditions. The standard free energy change (ΔG°') for ATP hydrolysis is approximately −30.5 kJ/mol. In the cytoplasm of a typical mammalian cell, the actual concentrations of ATP, ADP, and Pi result in an actual free energy change (ΔG) of approximately −54 kJ/mol. The biochemist notes that despite the large negative ΔG, ATP is kinetically stable in aqueous solution — a flask of ATP dissolved in water at pH 7 does not spontaneously hydrolyze at an appreciable rate without an enzyme catalyst.
questions.stem: The observation that ATP does not hydrolyze rapidly in solution despite a large negative ΔG is best explained by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The reaction has a high activation energy that prevents the thermodynamically favorable hydrolysis from proceeding at a measurable rate without enzymatic catalysis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable under standard conditions and only becomes favorable inside cells.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Water is a poor nucleophile at pH 7 and cannot attack the phosphoanhydride bond under any conditions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ATP hydrolysis is an endergonic reaction that requires energy input to proceed.

explanations.A: Thermodynamic favorability (negative ΔG) determines whether a reaction can occur spontaneously, but not how fast. ATP hydrolysis has a high activation energy barrier — the phosphoanhydride bond is kinetically resistant to nucleophilic attack by water. Enzymes (ATPases, kinases) lower this activation energy, allowing the thermodynamically favorable reaction to proceed at biologically relevant rates. This distinction between thermodynamics and kinetics is fundamental.
explanations.B: The passage states ΔG°' = −30.5 kJ/mol, which is already negative under standard biochemical conditions. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under both standard and cellular conditions — it is even more favorable in cells (ΔG = −54 kJ/mol).
explanations.C: Water can act as a nucleophile, and ATP hydrolysis does occur slowly in aqueous solution. The rate is just extremely slow compared to enzyme-catalyzed rates. Saying water 'cannot attack under any conditions' is too strong and incorrect.
explanations.D: ATP hydrolysis is exergonic (negative ΔG), not endergonic. An endergonic reaction has a positive ΔG and would not proceed spontaneously.

questions.stem: The difference between ΔG°' (−30.5 kJ/mol) and the cellular ΔG (−54 kJ/mol) is primarily due to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cellular temperature being higher than the standard temperature of 25°C

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cellular ATP concentrations being maintained far above equilibrium by continuous metabolic activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enzyme catalysis lowering the free energy of the products in cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: The presence of Mg²⁺ ions in the cytoplasm that alter the bond energies in ATP

explanations.A: While cellular temperature (~37°C) differs from standard temperature (25°C), this small difference does not account for the 23.5 kJ/mol discrepancy. The primary factor is the concentration term in the equation ΔG = ΔG°' + RT ln Q, not temperature.
explanations.B: The actual free energy change depends on reactant and product concentrations: ΔG = ΔG°' + RT ln([ADP][Pi]/[ATP]). Cells maintain ATP concentrations far above and ADP/Pi concentrations far below their equilibrium values through continuous metabolic regeneration of ATP. This makes the mass action ratio Q much smaller than Keq, driving ΔG more negative than ΔG°'.
explanations.C: Enzymes do not alter the free energy of reactants or products — they lower the activation energy of the transition state. The thermodynamic difference between ΔG°' and ΔG is determined entirely by concentration (the mass action ratio), not by enzyme activity.
explanations.D: Mg²⁺ coordination affects ATP's interactions and the effective concentration of free ATP, but the major contributor to the ΔG difference is the overall mass action ratio maintained by metabolism, not changes in bond energies from ion coordination.

questions.stem: If an enzyme catalyzing ATP hydrolysis lowers the activation energy by 40 kJ/mol, which of the following quantities also changes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The equilibrium constant for the reaction

choices.label: B
choices.text: The standard free energy change (ΔG°') of the reaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rate at which equilibrium is reached

choices.label: D
choices.text: The enthalpy change (ΔH) of the reaction

explanations.A: The equilibrium constant is determined by the standard free energy change: ΔG°' = −RT ln Keq. Enzymes do not alter ΔG°', so they cannot change Keq. Both catalyzed and uncatalyzed reactions reach the same equilibrium position.
explanations.B: ΔG°' is a thermodynamic state function determined by the difference in free energy between products and reactants. Enzymes affect the pathway (lowering the transition state energy) but not the endpoints. ΔG°' is unchanged.
explanations.C: Lowering the activation energy increases the rate constant (k) exponentially, as described by the Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^(−Ea/RT). A 40 kJ/mol reduction in Ea increases the rate by approximately e^(40000/(8.314×310)) ≈ 10^6.7 — millions of times faster. The equilibrium position is unchanged, but it is reached much more quickly.
explanations.D: ΔH is a state function determined by bond energies of reactants and products. Like ΔG°', it is independent of the reaction pathway and is not affected by enzyme catalysis.

questions.passage: A pharmacology researcher studies the kinetics of drug metabolism by a liver cytochrome P450 enzyme. The enzyme follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics with Km = 15 μM and Vmax = 200 nmol/min. At therapeutic doses, the drug concentration in the liver is approximately 2 μM. At toxic doses from overdose, the concentration can reach 150 μM. The researcher is concerned that at overdose levels, the enzyme may become saturated, leading to nonlinear accumulation of the drug.
questions.stem: At the therapeutic concentration of 2 μM, the reaction rate is approximately what fraction of Vmax?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1/2, because 2 μM is close to Km

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2/17, because the rate follows v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S])

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1/8, because 2 μM is roughly Km/8

choices.label: D
choices.text: 88%, because at 2 μM the enzyme is nearly saturated and operating close to Vmax

explanations.A: Km (15 μM) is not close to 2 μM. The rate equals Vmax/2 only when [S] = Km. At [S] = 2 μM, the substrate concentration is well below Km, so the enzyme is far from half-saturation.
explanations.B: Applying the Michaelis-Menten equation: v = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]) = 200 × 2/(15 + 2) = 400/17 ≈ 23.5 nmol/min. As a fraction of Vmax: 23.5/200 = 0.118, or about 12%. But 2/17 ≈ 0.118, which is 11.8% of Vmax. This is the correct calculation but the percentage described is slightly off due to rounding.
explanations.C: 2 μM is Km/7.5, not Km/8. Furthermore, the rate at [S] = Km/8 would be Vmax/(1+8) = Vmax/9 ≈ 11%, not 1/8 of Vmax (12.5%). This answer confuses the substrate concentration ratio with the rate fraction.
explanations.D: At [S] = 2 μM and Km = 15 μM, the substrate concentration is far below Km, so the enzyme is far from saturation. An enzyme is half-saturated at [S] = Km and approaches saturation only when [S] >> Km. At [S] = 2 μM, the enzyme has abundant spare capacity — it is operating at roughly 12% of Vmax, not 88%. This answer confuses a substrate concentration well below Km with near-saturation.

questions.stem: At the overdose concentration of 150 μM (10 × Km), the reaction rate is approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 10 × Vmax, because the concentration is 10 times Km

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vmax/2, because the enzyme is half-saturated at any concentration above Km

choices.label: C
choices.text: 10/11 of Vmax, because v = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]) = Vmax × 150/165

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vmax, because the enzyme is fully saturated at any concentration above Km

explanations.A: The rate can never exceed Vmax regardless of substrate concentration. Vmax is the theoretical maximum rate when every enzyme molecule is bound to substrate. Multiplying Vmax by any factor greater than 1 is impossible.
explanations.B: Half-saturation occurs only at [S] = Km. At [S] = 10Km, the enzyme is approximately 91% saturated, not 50%. The rate is much closer to Vmax than to Vmax/2.
explanations.C: v = Vmax × 150/(15+150) = Vmax × 150/165 = Vmax × 10/11 ≈ 0.91 × Vmax = 182 nmol/min. The enzyme is ~91% saturated — nearly at capacity but not fully saturated. This means small additional increases in drug concentration produce very small rate increases, leading to the nonlinear accumulation the researcher fears.
explanations.D: Full saturation (v = Vmax) requires [S] >> Km, approaching infinity. At [S] = 10Km, the enzyme is at 91% capacity, which is close but not equal to full saturation. The distinction matters pharmacologically because the remaining 9% capacity determines whether additional drug can be metabolized.

questions.stem: The researcher's concern about nonlinear drug accumulation at overdose levels reflects the transition from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero-order to first-order kinetics, where the rate becomes dependent on substrate concentration at high doses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reversible to irreversible binding, where the drug permanently inactivates the enzyme at high concentrations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Competitive to uncompetitive inhibition as excess drug blocks enzyme activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: First-order to zero-order kinetics, where the rate becomes independent of substrate concentration as the enzyme saturates

explanations.A: This is reversed. At low [S], the rate is proportional to [S] (first-order behavior). At high [S], the enzyme saturates and the rate plateaus near Vmax (zero-order behavior). The transition is from first-order to zero-order, not the reverse.
explanations.B: The passage describes enzyme saturation, not irreversible inactivation. The drug is still being metabolized — just not fast enough to keep up with the increased dose. Irreversible inactivation would mean the enzyme is permanently destroyed, which is a different pharmacological concern.
explanations.C: The passage does not describe inhibition — the drug is a substrate being metabolized, not an inhibitor. Enzyme saturation is a normal property of Michaelis-Menten kinetics at high substrate concentrations, not an inhibition mechanism.
explanations.D: At therapeutic doses ([S] << Km), the rate is approximately linear with [S]: v ≈ (Vmax/Km)[S], which is pseudo-first-order kinetics. At overdose levels ([S] >> Km), the enzyme is saturated and v ≈ Vmax regardless of [S], which is zero-order kinetics. In zero-order kinetics, doubling the dose does not double the clearance rate, causing nonlinear (disproportionate) drug accumulation.

questions.passage: A physical chemistry student measures the enthalpy change for the dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water. The student dissolves 8.0 g of NH₄NO₃ (molar mass = 80 g/mol) in 100 mL of water in a coffee-cup calorimeter. The temperature of the solution decreases from 25.0°C to 21.7°C. The specific heat capacity of the solution is assumed to be 4.18 J/(g·°C), and the density of the solution is approximately 1.0 g/mL.
questions.stem: Based on the calorimetry data, the enthalpy of dissolution of NH₄NO₃ per mole is approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: +25 kJ/mol (endothermic)

choices.label: B
choices.text: −25 kJ/mol (exothermic)

choices.label: C
choices.text: +14.9 kJ/mol (endothermic)

choices.label: D
choices.text: −14.9 kJ/mol (exothermic)

explanations.A: Let's calculate: q = mcΔT = (108 g)(4.18 J/g°C)(3.3°C) = 1,488 J ≈ 1.49 kJ. Moles of NH₄NO₃ = 8.0/80 = 0.10 mol. ΔH = 1.49/0.10 = 14.9 kJ/mol. Since the temperature decreased, the process is endothermic (positive ΔH). The value is +14.9 kJ/mol, not +25 kJ/mol.
explanations.B: The temperature decreased, indicating an endothermic process. An exothermic dissolution would increase the solution temperature. The sign is wrong.
explanations.C: q = mcΔT = (108 g)(4.18)(3.3) = 1,488 J. Moles = 0.10. ΔH = +1,488/0.10 = +14,880 J/mol ≈ +14.9 kJ/mol. The positive sign indicates endothermic dissolution — the solute absorbed heat from the water, cooling the solution. The lattice energy of NH₄NO₃ exceeds its hydration energy, so energy must be absorbed to separate the ions.
explanations.D: The magnitude (14.9 kJ/mol) is correct, but the sign is wrong. Temperature decrease = endothermic process = positive ΔH.

questions.stem: The dissolution of NH₄NO₃ is endothermic, yet it dissolves readily in water at room temperature. This observation indicates that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The dissolution must be driven by a sufficiently large increase in entropy (ΔS > 0) such that TΔS > ΔH, making ΔG negative.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The dissolution is thermodynamically unfavorable but proceeds because of rapid kinetics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The enthalpy change must actually be negative when measured more precisely.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Le Chatelier's principle drives the reaction forward at room temperature.

explanations.A: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. For a spontaneous process, ΔG < 0. With ΔH > 0 (endothermic), the only way ΔG can be negative is if TΔS > ΔH — the entropy increase must be large enough to overcome the unfavorable enthalpy. When NH₄NO₃ dissolves, the ordered crystal lattice breaks into dispersed, hydrated ions, producing a large entropy increase that drives dissolution despite the endothermic enthalpy.
explanations.B: If ΔG were positive, the dissolution would not be spontaneous regardless of kinetics. Kinetics determines how fast equilibrium is reached, not whether a process is favorable. The fact that NH₄NO₃ dissolves readily indicates negative ΔG, not favorable kinetics with unfavorable thermodynamics.
explanations.C: The calorimetry measurement clearly shows a temperature decrease, confirming endothermic dissolution. The positive ΔH is well-established for NH₄NO₃. There is no measurement error — the entropy term drives spontaneity.
explanations.D: Le Chatelier's principle describes how equilibria shift in response to perturbations (concentration, temperature, pressure changes). It does not explain why a process is thermodynamically favorable — that requires analysis of ΔG = ΔH − TΔS.

questions.stem: A reaction has ΔH = −80 kJ/mol and ΔS = −200 J/(mol·K). At which temperature range is this reaction spontaneous?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spontaneous at all temperatures because ΔH is negative

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spontaneous only above 400 K, when the TΔS term becomes sufficiently negative

choices.label: C
choices.text: Never spontaneous because both ΔH and ΔS are negative

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous only below 400 K, when the favorable enthalpy term dominates the unfavorable entropy term

explanations.A: A negative ΔH favors spontaneity, but ΔG also depends on the −TΔS term. With ΔS < 0 (negative), −TΔS is positive and grows with temperature. At sufficiently high temperatures, −TΔS will overwhelm ΔH and make ΔG positive (non-spontaneous).
explanations.B: Above 400 K, −TΔS = −(400)(−200/1000) = +80 kJ/mol. At exactly 400 K: ΔG = −80 + 80 = 0 (equilibrium). Above 400 K, ΔG becomes positive — the reaction is non-spontaneous. This answer gets the direction wrong.
explanations.C: Reactions with ΔH < 0 and ΔS < 0 are spontaneous at low temperatures where the enthalpy term dominates. They are not 'never spontaneous.' ΔG depends on both terms and on temperature.
explanations.D: Setting ΔG = 0: T = ΔH/ΔS = (−80,000)/(−200) = 400 K. Below 400 K, the magnitude of TΔS is smaller than |ΔH|, so the favorable enthalpy dominates: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS < 0. Above 400 K, the entropy penalty overcomes the enthalpy benefit and ΔG > 0. This reaction is spontaneous only at low temperatures.

questions.passage: A chemical engineer studies the Haber process for ammonia synthesis: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g), ΔH = −92 kJ/mol. The reaction is performed at 450°C and 200 atm. The engineer notes that the equilibrium constant at 450°C is relatively small (Keq ≈ 0.5), yet industrial plants achieve acceptable yields by continuously removing NH₃ from the reaction mixture.
questions.stem: The reaction is performed at 450°C despite the small Keq at that temperature. Why is a high temperature used for an exothermic reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Higher temperature increases Keq for exothermic reactions according to Le Chatelier's principle.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Higher temperature increases the rate of reaction by providing sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, even though it decreases Keq.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Higher temperature shifts the equilibrium toward products because there are fewer moles of gas on the product side.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher temperature is needed to liquefy the ammonia product for easier removal.

explanations.A: For exothermic reactions, Le Chatelier's principle predicts that increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward reactants (decreasing Keq). Higher temperature decreases the equilibrium yield of NH₃ — this is why Keq is small at 450°C.
explanations.B: The Haber process faces a kinetic-thermodynamic trade-off. Low temperatures favor equilibrium yield (larger Keq) but the reaction rate is impractically slow — the activation energy barrier means that at low temperatures, the system takes far too long to approach equilibrium. At 450°C, the rate is fast enough for industrial production, even though the equilibrium yield is lower. Continuous removal of NH₃ shifts equilibrium toward products, partially compensating.
explanations.C: Pressure, not temperature, exploits the difference in moles of gas. High pressure (200 atm) favors the product side (2 mol NH₃ vs. 4 mol reactant gas). Temperature and pressure have different effects and should not be conflated.
explanations.D: NH₃ is removed from the reaction mixture by cooling the exit gas, not by high-temperature liquefaction. The reaction temperature (450°C) is far above NH₃'s boiling point (−33°C). High temperature serves kinetic purposes, not separation purposes.

questions.topic: Le Chatelier's Principle / Kinetics vs Thermodynamics

questions.stem: The continuous removal of NH₃ from the reaction mixture increases the yield because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Removing product decreases Q below Keq, so the reaction shifts forward to re-establish equilibrium.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Removing product increases the activation energy for the reverse reaction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removing product changes the ΔH of the reaction, making it more exothermic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing product decreases the total pressure, which favors product formation.

explanations.A: The reaction quotient Q = [NH₃]²/([N₂][H₂]³). Removing NH₃ decreases the numerator, making Q < Keq. By Le Chatelier's principle (or equivalently, the thermodynamic drive to reach Q = Keq), the reaction shifts toward products to restore equilibrium. This is a standard application of Le Chatelier's principle to concentration changes.
explanations.B: Product removal does not change activation energies. Activation energy is a property of the reaction mechanism and transition state, not of the concentrations of reactants or products. Catalysts change Ea; concentration changes do not.
explanations.C: ΔH is a thermodynamic state function determined by the difference in bond energies between reactants and products. It does not change when product concentrations change. Removing product shifts the position of equilibrium, not the thermodynamic parameters.
explanations.D: Removing gaseous NH₃ does decrease total pressure, but in this system high pressure favors product formation (fewer moles of gas on the product side). A decrease in total pressure would actually shift equilibrium toward the reactant side (more moles of gas). The dominant effect of removing NH₃ is the concentration change (Q < Keq), not the pressure change.

questions.stem: A student measures the rate of a reaction at two temperatures and obtains the following data: at 300 K, k = 1.5 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹; at 310 K, k = 4.5 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹. The rate constant tripled with a 10 K increase. This temperature sensitivity is best explained by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The equilibrium constant shifts toward products at higher temperature.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A greater fraction of molecules has kinetic energy exceeding the activation energy at 310 K, as described by the Boltzmann distribution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reaction mechanism changes at higher temperatures, introducing a faster pathway.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased molecular collisions at higher temperature are solely responsible for the rate increase.

explanations.A: The rate constant k and the equilibrium constant Keq are different quantities. k describes the rate at which a reaction proceeds; Keq describes the ratio of products to reactants at equilibrium. Temperature affects both, but the tripling of k is a kinetic phenomenon explained by the Arrhenius equation, not by equilibrium shifts.
explanations.B: The Arrhenius equation (k = Ae^(−Ea/RT)) shows that k depends exponentially on temperature. A small increase in T significantly increases the fraction of molecules in the Boltzmann distribution that have energy ≥ Ea. For a 10 K increase from 300 to 310 K, the fraction of molecules exceeding typical biological activation energies can increase substantially, explaining the observed rate tripling.
explanations.C: Reaction mechanisms generally do not change over small temperature ranges. The same elementary steps occur at both temperatures; the difference is in the fraction of collisions with sufficient energy to surmount the activation barrier.
explanations.D: Increased collision frequency contributes to rate increases with temperature, but collision frequency increases by only ~1.7% for a 10 K rise (proportional to √T). The tripling of k is far too large to be explained by collisions alone — the exponential Boltzmann factor is the dominant effect.

questions.stem: For a first-order reaction with a half-life of 20 minutes, how long will it take for the concentration of reactant to decrease to 12.5% of its initial value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 40 minutes

choices.label: B
choices.text: 80 minutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: 60 minutes

choices.label: D
choices.text: 160 minutes

explanations.A: After 40 minutes (2 half-lives), the concentration is (1/2)² = 25% of the initial value, not 12.5%.
explanations.B: After 80 minutes (4 half-lives), the concentration is (1/2)⁴ = 6.25%, which is below 12.5%.
explanations.C: 12.5% = 1/8 = (1/2)³. Three half-lives are needed: 3 × 20 = 60 minutes. For first-order reactions, the half-life is constant (independent of concentration), so each successive halving takes the same amount of time.
explanations.D: After 160 minutes (8 half-lives), the concentration would be (1/2)⁸ = 0.39% — far below 12.5%.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the rate law and the equilibrium constant for an elementary reaction A ⇌ B with forward rate constant kf and reverse rate constant kr?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keq = kf + kr

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keq = kr/kf

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keq has no relationship to rate constants because thermodynamics and kinetics are completely independent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keq = kf/kr, because at equilibrium the forward and reverse rates are equal: kf[A] = kr[B], so [B]/[A] = kf/kr

explanations.A: The equilibrium constant is the ratio (not the sum) of rate constants. Adding rate constants has no physical meaning in this context.
explanations.B: This inverts the ratio. Keq = [products]/[reactants] = kf/kr. If Keq > 1 (products favored), then kf > kr — the forward rate constant must be larger. kr/kf would give the inverse of Keq.
explanations.C: While thermodynamics and kinetics describe different aspects of reactions, they are not completely independent for elementary reactions. The equilibrium constant is the ratio of forward and reverse rate constants, linking the two domains.
explanations.D: At equilibrium, the forward rate equals the reverse rate: kf[A]eq = kr[B]eq. Rearranging: [B]eq/[A]eq = kf/kr = Keq. This relationship holds for elementary reactions and connects the kinetic rate constants to the thermodynamic equilibrium constant.

questions.stem: A catalyst increases the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions of a reversible process by the same factor. Which of the following is a correct consequence of this property?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The catalyst shifts the equilibrium toward products because it accelerates the forward reaction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The catalyst changes ΔG° for the reaction because the transition state energy is lowered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The catalyst must increase the temperature of the system to accelerate both reactions equally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The catalyst allows the system to reach equilibrium more quickly without changing the equilibrium position.

explanations.A: A catalyst accelerates both forward and reverse reactions equally, so it does not shift the equilibrium position in either direction. If only the forward reaction were accelerated, the equilibrium would shift — but this would violate the principle of microscopic reversibility.
explanations.B: ΔG° is a state function determined by the free energies of reactants and products. The transition state energy affects kinetics (Ea), not thermodynamics (ΔG°). Lowering the transition state energy lowers the activation energy, increasing the rate without changing the equilibrium or ΔG°.
explanations.C: Catalysts do not work by increasing temperature. They provide an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. The reaction rate increases at the same temperature — no additional thermal energy is required.
explanations.D: Because the catalyst accelerates both forward and reverse reactions by the same factor, the equilibrium constant (Keq = kf/kr) remains unchanged. The system reaches the same equilibrium position but does so faster. This is the fundamental property of catalysis: kinetic enhancement without thermodynamic alteration.
